sources.title: antirez/kilo — a text editor in less than 1K lines of code
Writing an editor in less than 1000 lines of code, just for fun
Build Your Own Text Editor (snaptoken's step-by-step Kilo tutorial)
kilo.c (raw source) — main(), editorRefreshScreen, erow, editorOpen, editorSave, editorProcessKeypress
Text showdown: Gap Buffers vs Ropes — Core Dumped
termios(3) — Linux manual page (general terminal interface: ICANON, ECHO, VMIN, VTIME)
Digital VT100 User Guide, Chapter 3 — Programmer Information (escape/control sequences)
ANSI escape code — general reference for CSI/SGR/DEC-private-mode sequence semantics (ED, EL, CUP, DSR, SGR, DECTCEM)

structure.rationale: The concept list was fully specified by the requester (every entry is source:"user") and was kept as-is and in order. That order already mirrors the natural build sequence of kilo.c itself and the teaching sequence used by the snaptoken 'Build Your Own Text Editor' walkthrough of the same source: orient the reader on why a tiny editor is worth studying and what prerequisites they need, then build outward from the terminal (raw mode, escape sequences, the refresh loop) to the data model (editor state, text storage, navigation, editing), to persistence (open/save), to the higher-level features (search, syntax highlighting), to robustness (error handling, portability), and finally to a set of reflective/extension chapters (refactoring, adding features, testing, rebuilding from memory) that go beyond antirez's original ~1000 lines into general lessons about maintaining small C programs. This progression lets each chapter depend only on mechanisms already established in earlier ones, matching how kilo.c itself is organized top-to-bottom (terminal handling before the append buffer, the append buffer before the editor operations, editor operations before syntax highlighting).
structure.audience: Programmers who already know how to code and want to understand systems-programming fundamentals — raw terminal I/O, escape sequences, manual memory/string handling in C, and the discipline of keeping a real program small and correct — by reading a complete, working example instead of a toy snippet.

claims.text: Kilo's own source file opens with the line: "Kilo -- A very simple editor in less than 1-kilo lines of code (as counted by 'cloc'). Does not depend on libcurses, directly emits VT100 escapes on the terminal." — the name is a pun on the self-imposed size budget.

The kilo.c file shipped in the antirez/kilo repository is 1308 lines total (verified by direct download and wc -l), including the BSD-2-Clause license header and blank lines; cloc's stricter code-only count (comments and blank lines excluded) is what drops it under 1000, which is the qualifier the name actually refers to.

Kilo has zero external library dependencies — notably no ncurses/curses — and talks to the terminal directly by emitting VT100-style escape sequences; its #include list is entirely standard POSIX/libc headers (termios.h, unistd.h, sys/ioctl.h, fcntl.h, signal.h, etc.).

According to its own README, Kilo "was written in just a few hours taking code from my other two projects, load81 and linenoise" — it was assembled quickly from antirez's existing building blocks, not written from a blank slate.

The README explicitly frames Kilo as a teaching/starting-point project: "People are encouraged to use it as a starting point to write other editors or command line interfaces that are more advanced than the usual REPL style CLI." It is released under the BSD 2-clause license.

antirez's blog post title — "Writing an editor in less than 1000 lines of code, just for fun" — and secondary coverage of it place Kilo's origin around a period of renewed public discussion of GNU nano's status; antirez wrote Kilo as a for-fun, self-educational exercise rather than to replace or compete with an existing editor.

Despite its size budget, Kilo is a complete, usable editor: it opens and saves files, has incremental search (Ctrl-F), a status bar and message line, and single-language (C/C++) syntax highlighting driven by one small hardcoded table (HLDB) — evidence that 'tiny' did not mean 'toy'.

The community tutorial 'Build Your Own Text Editor' (snaptoken) walks the same program this book studies, step by step in C, explicitly using it as a vehicle to teach raw terminal-mode programming end-to-end — not as a series of disconnected exercises.

snippets.note: Verbatim opening comment of kilo.c, fetched directly from raw.githubusercontent.com/antirez/kilo/master/kilo.c on 2026-07-20 (file is 1308 lines total).

pitfalls: Don't let readers conflate 'less than 1K lines' with the literal file length — the qualifier is cloc's code-only count (comments/blank lines excluded); the shipped file is 1308 lines.

The 'tiny editor' framing can undersell scope — Kilo still covers raw terminal mode, VT100 escape sequences, manual dynamic-memory buffer management, and file I/O, which is real systems-programming ground, not a toy exercise.

claims.text: kilo.c's main() is a bare infinite loop of exactly two calls: editorRefreshScreen() then editorProcessKeypress(STDIN_FILENO), after one-time setup (initEditor, editorSelectSyntaxHighlight, editorOpen, enableRawMode). Every visible behavior of the editor is a consequence of this read-react-repaint cycle repeating.

editorProcessKeypress() blocks on a single read() of one keypress at a time (via editorReadKey), then dispatches on it with one big switch: distinct case labels exist for ENTER, CTRL_Q (quit), CTRL_S (save), CTRL_F (find), BACKSPACE/CTRL_H/DEL_KEY (delete), the four ARROW_* keys, PAGE_UP/PAGE_DOWN, CTRL_L, and ESC. There is no timer, no idle callback, no background thread — the whole program only does work in response to a keystroke.

Kilo's text model is one dynamically-growing C array of `erow` structs (one erow per line of the file), not a gap buffer, rope, or piece table. Each erow carries: idx (row index), size/chars (the raw line bytes, exactly as typed or loaded), rsize/render (a second buffer with tabs expanded to spaces, built only for display), hl (one highlight-type byte per rendered character), and hl_oc (whether the row ends inside an open multi-line comment).

The chars/render split inside erow exists so on-disk content and on-screen content can differ (tab expansion, and later syntax-highlight coloring) without the file itself ever holding anything but what the user actually typed — editing operates on chars; editorUpdateRow() regenerates render from chars after every change.

editorRefreshScreen() does not write escape sequences to the terminal one at a time. It accumulates every cursor-hide, redraw, status-bar, message-bar, and cursor-reposition escape into one in-memory append buffer (`struct abuf`), then flushes it with a single write() call — a batching pattern chosen specifically to avoid the flicker that many small writes would cause.

Persistence is two symmetrical operations: editorOpen() reads the file line-by-line with getline(), stripping the trailing newline/CR and calling editorInsertRow() for each line to build the erow array (a missing file is tolerated — ENOENT just starts an empty buffer); editorSave() calls editorRowsToString() to flatten the erow array back into one contiguous byte buffer, then opens/creates the file, ftruncate()s it to the new length, and writes the buffer in one write() call, clearing the dirty flag (E.dirty = 0) on success.

Kilo's own source comments on the risk in that save strategy: directly above the ftruncate+write, the code reads "Use truncate + a single write(2) call in order to make saving a bit safer, under the limits of what we can do in a small editor." It is a deliberate, named trade-off (not a safe, rename-based atomic save with a temp file) — antirez chose the simplest correct-enough approach rather than the more robust one, in keeping with the size budget.

Across text editors generally, the 'what must the buffer be' question has a well-known spectrum of answers with different trade-offs: a plain array of line-strings (simple, cheap for small files, costly for structural edits at scale) at one end; gap buffers (one contiguous allocation with a movable 'gap' at the edit point — fast for localized edits, and for files in the size range Kilo targets, faster in practice than a rope); ropes (a balanced tree of chunks giving O(log n) worst-case insert/delete/concatenate anywhere in the document, at the cost of real implementation complexity); and piece tables (index small immutable pieces instead of moving bytes, popular in editors that also need cheap unlimited undo). Kilo's plain array-of-erow-per-line sits at the simple end of this spectrum on purpose.

A gap buffer's advantage over a rope is locality: because real editing is usually clustered (you keep typing near where you last typed), a gap buffer's one-contiguous-allocation design outperforms a rope on small-to-medium files; ropes earn their complexity mainly at large scale or under non-local edit patterns (structural refactors, collaborative editing).

snippets.note: Verbatim main() from kilo.c, fetched 2026-07-20. The whole program is this loop plus one-time setup above it.
Verbatim erow struct from kilo.c, fetched 2026-07-20.
Core of editorSave(), verbatim including antirez's own comment acknowledging the trade-off, fetched 2026-07-20.

pitfalls: Don't let readers picture 'the buffer' as a single flat string of the whole file — Kilo's buffer is an array of per-line erow structs, and each erow itself holds two versions of its text (chars = raw, render = display-expanded), not one.

Don't let 'render' be misread as 'draw to the terminal' — in Kilo, render is a per-row *data* buffer (tabs expanded to spaces) built by editorUpdateRow(); the actual terminal write happens later, in editorRefreshScreen, via a completely separate append buffer (abuf).

Kilo's save is not crash-atomic (no write-to-temp-file-then-rename): a write() failure mid-save after ftruncate() can leave a shorter file than before. This is a real, named limitation, not a mistake to gloss over — good material to flag now and return to in the error-handling and portability chapters.

The read-react-repaint loop is strictly synchronous and single-threaded: editorProcessKeypress blocks on read() until a key arrives, so nothing else (no autosave timer, no background reflow) can happen between keystrokes. That's a defining constraint of Kilo's architecture, not an incidental detail.

claims.text: Kilo keeps its entire editor state in one global, a single static struct instance `static struct editorConfig E;` holding cursor position, scroll offsets, screen size, the row array, the dirty flag, the open filename, the status message, and the current syntax pointer. Nearly every function in kilo.c reads or writes fields of this one struct instead of having state threaded through parameters — the whole program is organized around one shared mutable record.

kilo.c is plain C, and its very first non-comment lines are feature-test macros — `#ifdef __linux__` / `#define _POSIX_C_SOURCE 200809L` — that ask glibc to expose POSIX.1-2008 library functions (such as getline()) that a strict-ISO-C build would otherwise hide. This is a real, necessary line in the file, not boilerplate to skip past.

Kilo's key constants are a plain C enum (`enum KEY_ACTION`) whose Ctrl-key entries are pre-computed literal ASCII values: CTRL_C=3, CTRL_D=4, CTRL_F=6, CTRL_H=8, CTRL_L=12, CTRL_Q=17, CTRL_S=19, CTRL_U=21, plus ESC=27 and BACKSPACE=127 alongside symbolic values (1000+) for arrow/page/home/end/delete keys that don't correspond to any single received byte.

Those Ctrl-key literals are not arbitrary: on an ASCII terminal, holding Ctrl while typing a letter clears bits 5 and 6 of that letter's ASCII code — equivalent to `letter & 0x1F`. 'Q' is 0x51 (binary 0101 0001); 0x51 & 0x1F = 0x11 = 17, which is exactly CTRL_Q. The popular community walkthrough of this same program (snaptoken's 'Build Your Own Text Editor') names this relationship explicitly with a macro, `#define CTRL_KEY(k) ((k) & 0x1F)` — antirez's shipped kilo.c reaches the identical numbers by hand instead of computing them, which is why a reader who has only seen the tutorial's macro can be surprised not to find it in the real source.

Terminal I/O in kilo.c goes through raw POSIX syscalls, not buffered stdio: editorReadKey() calls read(fd,&c,1) directly on a file descriptor (STDIN_FILENO), and every screen update goes out through a single write() call — kilo.c does not use getchar()/putchar()/printf() for the terminal's interactive path.

Kilo controls the terminal through the termios API: enableRawMode() calls tcgetattr() to fetch the current struct termios into a local copy, clears flags like ECHO, ICANON, ISIG, and IXON with bitwise AND-NOT (`c_lflag &= ~(...)`), sets CS8 with bitwise OR (`c_cflag |= (CS8)`), and applies the modified struct with tcsetattr(fd, TCSAFLUSH, &raw). The direction of the operator — clearing vs. setting — is the entire meaning of each line.

By POSIX default, a terminal is in canonical mode (ICANON set): the kernel's line discipline buffers keystrokes, handles backspace/line-editing itself, and only hands a full line to the reading program once Enter (or an EOL/EOF character) is typed. Turning ICANON off switches to noncanonical ('raw') input, where read() can return individual bytes as they arrive; ECHO (on by default) controls whether typed characters are echoed back to the screen by the terminal driver; and in noncanonical mode, c_cc[VMIN] and c_cc[VTIME] together decide how many bytes (or how long) read() waits before returning.

Kilo asks the kernel for the terminal's size with `ioctl(1, TIOCGWINSZ, &ws)`, and if that fails or reports zero columns (which happens on some terminals/multiplexers), falls back to moving the cursor to the bottom-right corner with a raw escape sequence and reading back its reported row/column position instead.

Kilo grows its C arrays one element at a time with realloc(), always reassigning the pointer to whatever (possibly relocated) block realloc() returns: editorInsertRow() does `E.row = realloc(E.row, sizeof(erow)*(E.numrows+1))` every time a line is added, and abAppend() (the screen-write append buffer) does `ab->b = realloc(ab->b, ab->len+len)` every time text is appended to a frame.

Kilo's C strings follow the ordinary convention (a char* to the first byte plus a trailing '\0'), but the code is careful to also track an explicit byte length alongside the pointer (erow.size/rsize, abuf.len) rather than relying solely on strlen(), because it copies bytes with memcpy()/memmove() (which take an explicit length and are binary-safe) rather than strcpy().

Kilo represents a syntax definition (struct editorSyntax) with two NULL-terminated arrays of C strings — `char **filematch` and `char **keywords` — the standard C idiom for a variable-length list of strings: code walks the array comparing/matching entries until it reaches a NULL pointer, since C arrays carry no built-in length.

editorSetStatusMessage() is declared `void editorSetStatusMessage(const char *fmt, ...)` — a variadic function using the same <stdarg.h> triad (va_start/vsnprintf/va_end) that printf() itself is built on — so callers write printf-style calls like editorSetStatusMessage("%d lines", n), and vsnprintf formats safely into the fixed E.statusmsg[80] buffer, truncating rather than overflowing it if the message is too long.

Kilo's error handling is direct and non-exceptional (C has no exceptions): functions that can fail return -1, often via a `goto fatal:` label that first sets errno; unrecoverable startup failures instead call perror() (print the C library's text for the current errno) followed by exit(1) — e.g. failing to open a file, or failing to query the terminal's size.

VT100-style escape sequences (used from Chapter 05 onward for cursor movement, clearing, and color) are ordinary bytes handed to write() — the terminal emulator, not the C program, is what interprets a sequence starting with ESC (0x1B) followed by '[' and reacts by moving its cursor or repainting. Kilo never draws a pixel or 'controls' the screen directly; it only ever writes text the terminal has agreed to treat specially.

snippets.code:
enum KEY_ACTION{
        KEY_NULL = 0,
        CTRL_C = 3,         /* Ctrl-c */
        CTRL_D = 4,         /* Ctrl-d */
        CTRL_F = 6,         /* Ctrl-f */
        CTRL_H = 8,         /* Ctrl-h */
        TAB = 9,            /* Tab */
        CTRL_L = 12,        /* Ctrl+l */
        ENTER = 13,         /* Enter */
        CTRL_Q = 17,        /* Ctrl-q */
        CTRL_S = 19,        /* Ctrl-s */
        CTRL_U = 21,        /* Ctrl-u */
        ESC = 27,           /* Escape */
        BACKSPACE =  127,   /* Backspace */
        /* The following are just soft codes, not really reported by the
         * terminal directly. */
        ARROW_LEFT = 1000,
        ARROW_RIGHT,
        ARROW_UP,
        ARROW_DOWN,
        DEL_KEY,
        HOME_KEY,
        END_KEY,
        PAGE_UP,
        PAGE_DOWN
};
snippets.note: Verbatim enum from kilo.c (lines ~114-138), fetched from raw.githubusercontent.com/antirez/kilo/master/kilo.c on 2026-07-20. Ctrl values are literal, pre-computed ASCII codes, not a macro.
Core of enableRawMode() in kilo.c, fetched 2026-07-20. &= ~(...) clears flags, |= (...) sets them.
Verbatim from kilo.c, fetched 2026-07-20. A variadic function built on the same stdarg.h triad as printf().
Verbatim abAppend() from kilo.c, fetched 2026-07-20 — the realloc-then-reassign growth pattern used throughout the file.
Verbatim struct from kilo.c, fetched 2026-07-20. filematch and keywords are NULL-terminated arrays of char*.

pitfalls: Readers who learned Ctrl-key handling from the popular snaptoken tutorial may expect a `CTRL_KEY(k)` macro; antirez's actual shipped kilo.c has no such macro and instead hardcodes the already-computed literal values in `enum KEY_ACTION`. Both are correct and produce the same numbers — the difference is only which file you're looking at.

`c_lflag &= ~(...)` (AND-NOT: clears/disables the listed flags) and `c_cflag |= (...)` (OR: sets/enables the listed flags) appear right next to each other in enableRawMode() — misreading the operator direction inverts the meaning of the whole line.

realloc() may return a different address than it was given, or fail and return NULL. Every correct call site in kilo.c immediately reassigns the result back into the owning pointer (and kilo.c doesn't handle the NULL-return failure case beyond returning early in abAppend()) — never keep using the old pointer, and never assume the block didn't move.

Escape sequences are inert bytes to the C program itself — kilo.c has no direct access to 'the screen'. If this isn't clear yet, Chapter 05 (Escape Sequences and Screen Drawing) will read as magic instead of as syscalls plus a cooperating terminal emulator.

claims.text: enableRawMode(fd) guards itself three ways before touching the terminal: it returns immediately (0) if E.rawmode is already set (idempotent — safe to call more than once), it jumps to a `fatal:` label (errno=ENOTTY, return -1) if isatty(STDIN_FILENO) is false (stdin isn't a terminal at all, e.g. input is piped from a file), and only then does it register `atexit(editorAtExit)` and call tcgetattr(fd,&orig_termios) to snapshot the terminal's current settings into a file-scope static, before ever modifying anything.

`static struct termios orig_termios;` is declared separately from `struct editorConfig E` — it is not editor state, it is terminal-restoration bookkeeping: the one snapshot needed to put the user's shell back exactly the way it was, taken once, before the first modification.

enableRawMode copies the snapshot (`raw = orig_termios`) and mutates the copy, never the original, then clears/sets specific flags with the same &=~(...) / |=(...) bitwise idiom used everywhere else in the file: input flags BRKINT, ICRNL, INPCK, ISTRIP, IXON are cleared (kilo.c's own comment: "no break, no CR to NL, no parity check, no strip char, no start/stop output control"); the output flag OPOST is cleared ("disable post processing" — the driver's automatic newline translation); CS8 is OR'd into the control flags (8-bit characters); and the local flags ECHO, ICANON, IEXTEN, ISIG are cleared (kilo.c's own comment: "echoing off, canonical off, no extended functions, no signal chars (^Z,^C)").

Clearing ISIG specifically stops the terminal driver from turning Ctrl-C/Ctrl-Z into SIGINT/SIGTSTP before Kilo ever sees the byte — without it, Ctrl-C would kill the editor instead of arriving as a plain byte Kilo's own key-dispatch (CTRL_C=3 in enum KEY_ACTION) could act on.

c_cc[VMIN]=0 and c_cc[VTIME]=1 together configure read()'s blocking behavior in noncanonical mode: return as soon as at least 0 bytes are available — i.e. return immediately if a byte has arrived — otherwise return after a 100ms timeout (VTIME's unit is tenths of a second) with a result of 0 bytes read, rather than blocking forever. Kilo's own comment on the VMIN line: "Return each byte, or zero for timeout."

tcsetattr(fd, TCSAFLUSH, &raw) is called with TCSAFLUSH specifically, which applies the new settings only after flushing (discarding) any input that has been typed but not yet read — so keystrokes buffered before raw mode takes effect don't leak through and get misinterpreted once it does.

disableRawMode(fd) is a guarded, deliberately best-effort restore: `if (E.rawmode) { tcsetattr(fd,TCSAFLUSH,&orig_termios); E.rawmode = 0; }`, with a comment directly above it — "Don't even check the return value as it's too late." — because by the time this runs (at exit) there is no useful recovery action left if tcsetattr itself fails.

editorAtExit(void) is a one-line wrapper — `disableRawMode(STDIN_FILENO);` — registered with the C standard library's atexit() inside enableRawMode itself. atexit() guarantees the registered function runs whenever the process calls exit() (including an exit() reached from deep inside error-handling code, e.g. a `goto fatal` elsewhere in the program) or returns normally from main(); it does not run on a signal that isn't handled (e.g. an unhandled SIGSEGV) or on a hard kill.

editorReadKey(fd) blocks in a small loop — `while ((nread = read(fd,&c,1)) == 0);` — that ignores VMIN/VTIME timeouts (nread==0) and keeps retrying, and only treats nread==-1 (a genuine read error) as fatal, calling exit(1). This is the direct consequence of the VMIN=0/VTIME=1 setting: a timeout is not an error, it's just 'nothing typed yet,' and the loop is what turns Kilo's technically-non-blocking-with-timeout read() back into an interactively-blocking single-keypress read from the caller's point of view.

Once one byte `c` is read, editorReadKey enters a switch: the `default` case returns the plain byte c immediately (any ordinary character or Ctrl-key byte). The `case ESC:` branch is where multi-byte sequences are decoded — it reads up to two more bytes with the same short-timeout read(), and if either of those reads returns 0 (times out), the function returns plain ESC. This is exactly how Kilo distinguishes a lone Esc keypress from the first byte of an arrow-key sequence: not a special terminator byte, but whether the next bytes arrive within the VTIME window.

For sequences starting `ESC [` (the CSI introducer), editorReadKey branches again on the next byte: 'A'/'B'/'C'/'D' map directly to ARROW_UP/ARROW_DOWN/ARROW_RIGHT/ARROW_LEFT, and 'H'/'F' map to HOME_KEY/END_KEY. If instead that byte is a digit ('0'-'9'), it's an 'extended' sequence — a third byte is read, and if that third byte is '~', the digit selects DEL_KEY ('3'), PAGE_UP ('5'), or PAGE_DOWN ('6'). A separate `ESC O` prefix (seq[0]=='O') is also checked for 'H'/'F' → HOME_KEY/END_KEY, because different terminal emulators encode Home/End with either the CSI (`ESC [ H`) or SS3 (`ESC O H`) introducer.

Every branch of editorReadKey's escape-sequence decoding returns one of the enum KEY_ACTION 'soft codes' (ARROW_UP, DEL_KEY, PAGE_UP, HOME_KEY, …, all numbered 1000 and above) — values that were never actually sent as a single byte by any terminal, but that let editorProcessKeypress's switch statement treat a 3-byte escape sequence and a single printable byte identically: as one int returned from one function call.

snippets.note: Verbatim enableRawMode/disableRawMode/editorAtExit + the orig_termios global, fetched from raw.githubusercontent.com/antirez/kilo/master/kilo.c on 2026-07-20.
Verbatim editorReadKey from kilo.c, fetched from raw.githubusercontent.com/antirez/kilo/master/kilo.c on 2026-07-20.

pitfalls: Forgetting to restore the terminal (no atexit-registered disableRawMode) is a classic from-scratch-terminal-program bug: if the process exits without restoring ICANON/ECHO, the user's shell is left unreadable (no echo, no line editing) until they run `reset` or `stty sane` blind.

With VMIN=0/VTIME=1, read() returning 0 means 'timed out, no byte available' — not EOF and not an error. Only nread==-1 is a real error in editorReadKey's loop; code that treats a 0 return as end-of-input would misbehave constantly (a timeout happens on every idle 100ms tick).

Distinguishing a lone Esc keypress from the first byte of an arrow-key/function-key escape sequence is purely timing-based in this design: it relies on the terminal delivering the remaining bytes of the sequence (e.g. `[` then `A`) within the VTIME window after the initial ESC. This is why VTIME is set to a short-but-nonzero value rather than 0 — it's the mechanism, not an incidental choice.

Home/End are checked under two different escape prefixes (`ESC [ H`/`ESC [ F` and `ESC O H`/`ESC O F`) because real terminal emulators disagree on which introducer they use for those two keys; handling only one prefix breaks Home/End on some terminals.

enableRawMode's guard order matters: it checks `E.rawmode` (idempotency) and `isatty()` (is this actually a terminal) before calling atexit() and tcgetattr() — calling tcgetattr on a non-tty file descriptor, or registering the exit handler before confirming there's anything to restore, would be wrong.

claims.text: kilo.c never links against curses/terminfo and never asks the OS to draw anything — every visual effect (moving the cursor, erasing text, coloring a character, hiding the cursor) is produced by writing plain ASCII bytes to stdout that happen to start with ESC (0x1B) followed by '['; the terminal emulator on the other end recognizes that prefix (the Control Sequence Introducer, CSI) and reacts to what follows. The C program has no more direct access to 'the screen' than it does when printing an ordinary string.

editorRefreshScreen() does not send a single full-screen clear (ESC[2J / ED) anywhere in kilo.c — a direct grep of the shipped source for '[2J' finds zero matches. Instead it hides the cursor, homes it with ESC[H, then rewrites every visible row from the top every single call, erasing only to the end of each line (ESC[0K) as it goes; because every row is redrawn every frame, there is nothing left over from the previous frame that a full clear would need to remove.

This differs from the widely-read community walkthrough of the same program: the snaptoken 'Build Your Own Text Editor' tutorial teaches an initial ESC[2J clear-the-whole-screen step early on. antirez's actual shipped kilo.c takes the leaner, flicker-avoiding route of never clearing wholesale — worth naming explicitly so a reader who has seen the tutorial isn't confused not to find ESC[2J in the real file.

The whole frame is assembled in one heap-growing 'append buffer' (struct abuf { char *b; int len; }) before a single byte reaches the terminal. kilo.c's own comment on the struct: "We define a very simple 'append buffer' structure, that is an heap allocated string where we can append to. This is useful in order to write all the escape sequences in a buffer and flush them to the standard output in a single call, to avoid flickering effects." abAppend() grows the buffer with realloc() (the same realloc-then-reassign pattern as erow); abFree() releases it; editorRefreshScreen() finishes with exactly one write(STDOUT_FILENO, ab.b, ab.len) and one abFree(&ab).

editorRefreshScreen()'s very first two writes into the append buffer are ESC[?25l (DECTCEM: hide the text cursor) and ESC[H (CUP with no parameters: home the cursor to row 1, column 1). The cursor stays hidden for the entire redraw and is only shown again — ESC[?25h — as the last thing appended, positioned at its true final coordinates. The user never sees a half-drawn frame or the cursor jumping through intermediate positions; they only ever see it appear once, in its correct final spot.

Rows with no file content past E.numrows print a bare '~' (a convention borrowed from vi-family editors for 'this line does not exist in the file'), except that when the file is completely empty, one row (E.screenrows/3, roughly a third of the way down) instead centers a 'Kilo editor -- verison %s' welcome banner by padding it with spaces computed from E.screencols; every such line is still terminated with ESC[0K\r\n — erase-to-end-of-line before the carriage return/newline, so any leftover character from whatever was on screen before this redraw can't survive at the row's tail.

Text-row coloring is emitted lazily, not per-character: editorRefreshScreen tracks current_color across the row's characters and only writes an SGR color sequence (ESC[<n>m, via editorSyntaxToColor()'s mapping — comment=36 cyan, keyword1=33 yellow, keyword2=32 green, string=35 magenta, number=31 red, search-match=34 blue, default=37 white) when the color actually changes, and writes ESC[39m (reset foreground to default) when transitioning back to HL_NORMAL. A run of ten same-colored characters costs one color escape, not ten.

Non-printable bytes (HL_NONPRINT, e.g. raw control characters that survived into a line) get a distinct visual treatment from color: they're wrapped in ESC[7m (SGR reverse/negative video — swap foreground and background) then ESC[0m (full attribute reset), rendering as an inverse-video glyph — '@'+c for codes ≤26 (so Ctrl-A shows as a reversed '@'+1='A') or '?' otherwise — instead of silently corrupting the terminal's own display state the way an unescaped raw control byte could.

Each rendered text row still ends with its own ESC[39m (reset color) + ESC[0K (erase to end of line) + \r\n, independent of the per-character color resets above — belt-and-suspenders against a row ending mid-color-escape leaking into the next line, and against a shorter new row leaving stale characters from a longer previous row at that same screen position.

Below the text rows, editorRefreshScreen appends two more rows built from the same buffer: a status row in reverse video (ESC[7m ... ESC[0m\r\n) holding '<filename> - <N> lines (modified)' left-aligned and 'currentline/totallines' right-aligned, space-padded between them to exactly E.screencols; then a message row (plain, ESC[0K first) that only shows E.statusmsg if it was set less than 5 seconds ago (time(NULL)-E.statusmsg_time < 5) — an old message silently disappears on the next redraw instead of needing to be explicitly cleared.

The final escape written every frame is the cursor-positioning one: snprintf(buf,32,"\x1b[%d;%dH",E.cy+1,cx) — CUP, 1-indexed because terminals count rows/columns from 1 while E.cy is 0-indexed. cx is not simply E.cx+1: a loop walks the row from E.coloff up to E.cx counting a TAB as advancing to the next multiple of 8 (cx += 7-(cx%8) then cx++) so the escape sequence places the cursor at the character's true on-screen column, not its byte offset into the row — the same chars/render distinction from Chapter 02 showing up again here, this time in cursor math instead of drawing.

Escape sequences aren't only for drawing — Kilo also uses one to ask the terminal a question. getCursorPosition() writes ESC[6n (DSR, Device Status Report requesting the cursor position) and then reads the terminal's reply directly off the input file descriptor, byte by byte, until it sees 'R': the reply has the form ESC[rows;colsR, parsed with sscanf(buf+2,"%d;%d",rows,cols) after confirming buf[0]==ESC and buf[1]=='['. This reuses the exact same read() the editor otherwise uses for keystrokes — for a brief moment editorReadKey's usual job is done by hand, to read a terminal reply instead of a keypress.

getWindowSize() prefers ioctl(1, TIOCGWINSZ, &ws) (an OS-level query with no escape sequences involved) and only falls back to escape-sequence probing if that ioctl fails or reports ws_col==0 (which the code notes happens on some terminals/multiplexers). The fallback: write ESC[999C ESC[999B (cursor-forward 999, cursor-down 999 — deliberately oversized amounts, since real VT100-family terminals clamp cursor motion at the screen edge rather than wrapping or erroring) to shove the cursor into the bottom-right corner regardless of actual size, call getCursorPosition() to read back where it landed (= the screen's row/column count), then restore the cursor to wherever it started with a computed ESC[%d;%dH before returning — leaving the visible cursor position unchanged by a size query the user never asked for.

updateWindowSize() calls getWindowSize() and then does E.screenrows -= 2, permanently reserving the bottom two terminal rows for the status bar and message bar computed in editorRefreshScreen — the text-drawing loop's 'for (y = 0; y < E.screenrows; y++)' never has a chance to overwrite them because they were subtracted out of screenrows before that loop ever runs. handleSigWinCh(), registered with signal(SIGWINCH, handleSigWinCh), re-runs updateWindowSize() whenever the kernel reports the terminal was resized, and clamps E.cy back inside the new bounds — so escape-sequence-based drawing and the ioctl/escape-sequence-based size query stay consistent even if the user resizes their terminal window mid-session.

CTRL_L ('Ctrl-L', the conventional 'refresh/redraw screen' keystroke in many terminal programs) is handled in editorProcessKeypress's switch as a literal empty case with only a comment: "Just refresht [sic] the line as side effect." No escape sequence is emitted specially for it, because editorRefreshScreen() already runs unconditionally on every single trip through the main loop (main()'s while(1) calls it before every keypress is even read) — Ctrl-L doesn't need to trigger a redraw because a redraw was always about to happen regardless.

pitfalls: Readers coming from the snaptoken tutorial will expect an initial ESC[2J full-screen clear; the real kilo.c has none anywhere in the file. Presenting ESC[2J as 'how Kilo clears the screen' would be inventing behavior the shipped source doesn't have — it clears incrementally, per line, every frame, instead.

The cursor is hidden (ESC[?25l) before the frame is drawn and only shown again (ESC[?25h) after the cursor's final position is written — in that order. Reversing the order, or omitting the hide, reintroduces the exact flicker/cursor-jumping the whole append-buffer design exists to avoid.

ESC[6n's reply (ESC[rows;colsR) arrives on the same file descriptor Kilo normally reads keystrokes from. getCursorPosition() must consume exactly that reply, byte by byte until 'R', before any other read() call — code that called editorReadKey() at the wrong moment during a getWindowSize() fallback could misinterpret the terminal's size reply as a keypress.

ESC[999C / ESC[999B are not 'move to column/row 999' on a screen that large — they rely on VT100-family terminals clamping cursor motion at the actual screen edge r's boundary. Read them as 'move by up to 999, but no further than the edge', not as absolute positioning.

cx in the final cursor-positioning escape is deliberately not E.cx+1 — it re-walks the row expanding TABs exactly as editorUpdateRow()'s render buffer does, so the terminal cursor lands on the same visual column as the rendered text. Using E.cx directly would misplace the terminal cursor on any row containing a tab.

CTRL_L looking like a no-op in the switch statement is easy to misread as 'Kilo ignores Ctrl-L' — it doesn't; the keystroke is consumed (so it doesn't fall through to insertion) and the redraw it would have requested is already guaranteed by the unconditional per-loop editorRefreshScreen() call, so no extra action is needed.

claims.text: main()'s full body is: an argc!=2 usage-and-exit check, then one-time setup (initEditor(); editorSelectSyntaxHighlight(argv[1]); editorOpen(argv[1]); enableRawMode(STDIN_FILENO); editorSetStatusMessage("HELP: Ctrl-S = save | Ctrl-Q = quit | Ctrl-F = find");), then exactly `while(1) { editorRefreshScreen(); editorProcessKeypress(STDIN_FILENO); }`. After setup, the entire remaining lifetime of the program is that two-call loop, run unconditionally forever.

The order inside the loop is deliberate, not arbitrary: editorRefreshScreen() runs before editorProcessKeypress() on every iteration, so each pass first paints whatever the previous iteration's keypress produced, then blocks waiting for and reacting to the next one. The very first iteration paints the editor's just-opened, unedited state before any key has been read at all.

editorReadKey(fd) — called from inside editorProcessKeypress — is what actually blocks the loop: `while ((nread = read(fd,&c,1)) == 0);` loops on read() returning 0, which (per Chapter 04's VMIN=0/VTIME=1 termios setting) means 'no byte arrived within the 100ms timeout', not an error or EOF. Because a timeout is silently retried rather than treated as an event, the process spends the 'read' half of each cycle repeatedly sleeping through short syscalls instead of spinning a busy loop.

Only nread==-1 (a genuine read() error) is fatal in editorReadKey, calling exit(1) directly — there is no other exit path out of the blocking wait; the loop's 'react' half only ever begins once a real byte (or escape sequence) has actually arrived.

editorProcessKeypress(fd) dispatches the one key it read through a single switch(c): ENTER inserts a newline, CTRL_C is explicitly a no-op (own comment: "We ignore ctrl-c, it can't be so simple to lose the changes to the edited file."), CTRL_Q quits (with a confirmation gate, see below), CTRL_S saves, CTRL_F starts find, BACKSPACE/CTRL_H/DEL_KEY all call editorDelChar(), PAGE_UP/PAGE_DOWN snap the cursor to the top/bottom of the screen and then call editorMoveCursor() E.screenrows times in a loop (paging is implemented as many single-line moves reusing the arrow-key function, not a separate scroll routine), the four ARROW_* keys call editorMoveCursor(c) directly, CTRL_L and ESC are both empty no-op cases, and default (anything not explicitly listed) calls editorInsertChar(c) — every unrecognized byte is treated as literal insertable text, there is no separate 'is this printable' check.

Ctrl-Q's quit confirmation is gated by `#define KILO_QUIT_TIMES 3` and `static int quit_times = KILO_QUIT_TIMES;` declared *inside* editorProcessKeypress as a function-local static — it is deliberately not a field of the global `struct editorConfig E`, even though almost everything else stateful in Kilo lives there. The CTRL_Q case checks `if (E.dirty && quit_times)`: if the file has unsaved changes and the countdown hasn't reached zero, it prints a warning ('Press Ctrl-Q %d more times to quit.'), decrements quit_times, and returns without exiting; otherwise (file is clean, or the countdown already reached zero) it calls exit(0) directly.

The very last statement in editorProcessKeypress, after the entire switch and unconditional for any key that reaches it, is `quit_times = KILO_QUIT_TIMES; /* Reset it to the original value. */` — so pressing any key other than Ctrl-Q (including ones that do nothing observable, like ESC or CTRL_L) resets the countdown. Quitting an unsaved file requires pressing Ctrl-Q three times in a row with nothing else typed in between, not three times total over the session.

initEditor() — called once, before enableRawMode() and before the loop — zeroes/nulls every field of E (cursor position, scroll offsets, row count/array, dirty flag, filename, syntax pointer), then immediately calls updateWindowSize() and registers `signal(SIGWINCH, handleSigWinCh)`. Screen dimensions (E.screenrows/E.screencols) are therefore already valid before the very first editorRefreshScreen() call in the loop ever runs.

handleSigWinCh() is installed via plain signal(), not sigaction() with SA_RESTART, and its body calls updateWindowSize() (re-querying and re-reserving the two status rows) and then editorRefreshScreen() directly, followed by clamping E.cy back inside the new bounds. A live terminal resize therefore triggers a repaint from inside signal-handler context, asynchronously with respect to the loop's own scheduled editorRefreshScreen() call — the one documented case in kilo.c where a redraw happens outside the loop's normal read-then-paint rhythm.

pitfalls: It's tempting to picture the loop as 'repaint only when something changed' or 'read all pending keys, then repaint once' — it does neither. Every single iteration does exactly one editorReadKey()-driven read and exactly one full editorRefreshScreen(), unconditionally, whether or not that key changed anything visible.

quit_times is easy to assume lives in E alongside everything else stateful — it deliberately doesn't. It's a function-local static inside editorProcessKeypress, which matters for Chapter 07's 'almost everything lives in one struct' framing: this is the one notable exception.

The quit-confirmation counter resets on *any* key, not just 'wrong' ones — pressing Ctrl-Q, then accidentally an arrow key, then Ctrl-Q again starts the count over at 3, not 2. Readers who assume it's a total across the session will misjudge how many Ctrl-Q presses quitting actually takes.

SIGWINCH's handler calls editorRefreshScreen() directly from signal-handler context, outside the main loop's own call — this is a real, deliberate exception to 'repaints only happen once per loop iteration' and worth naming explicitly rather than letting the reader assume the loop is the only place a repaint can be triggered from.

claims.text: kilo.c defines exactly two structs before the editor's global state: `typedef struct erow {...} erow;` (a single file line: idx, size, rsize, chars, render, hl, hl_oc) and `struct editorSyntax {...}` (the current syntax-highlight ruleset: filematch, keywords, comment delimiters, flags). Everything else the editor needs to remember lives in one further struct, `struct editorConfig`.

struct editorConfig's exact fields, in source order, are: `int cx, cy;` (cursor position in characters), `int rowoff;` (row scroll offset), `int coloff;` (column scroll offset), `int screenrows;` / `int screencols;` (visible terminal size), `int numrows;` (row count), `int rawmode;` (is the terminal currently in raw mode), `erow *row;` (the heap-allocated array of rows), `int dirty;` (unsaved-changes flag), `char *filename;` (currently open file), `char statusmsg[80];` and `time_t statusmsg_time;` (the status-bar message and when it was set), and `struct editorSyntax *syntax;` (the active highlighter, or NULL).

The single instance is declared at file scope directly under the struct definition: `static struct editorConfig E;`. The `static` here is a C internal-linkage qualifier restricting E's visibility to kilo.c's one translation unit (the whole program is one file) — it is a deliberate encapsulation choice, not an accident of C requiring globals to be static. Being a plain (non-pointer) file-scope struct, E is zero-initialized by the C runtime before main() ever runs, and initEditor() then overwrites every field explicitly anyway.

Every one of kilo.c's ~30 editor* functions reads or writes E directly by name — there is no editor handle, context pointer, or struct parameter threaded through calls (contrast editorProcessKeypress(int fd), which takes fd as its own parameter but still reaches into E for cursor/dirty/screen state). This is a single-instance, process-global design: Kilo can only ever edit one file in one terminal per process, and that assumption is baked into E being a bare global rather than a heap-allocated, passable value.

E.rawmode is the one editorConfig field whose job is entirely about terminal-restore safety, not about the document being edited: enableRawMode() sets it to 1 only after tcsetattr() actually succeeds, and disableRawMode() checks `if (E.rawmode)` before calling tcsetattr() to restore orig_termios, then resets it to 0. Because disableRawMode() is registered once via `atexit(editorAtExit)`, this flag is what lets cleanup-on-exit be idempotent and safe to call even if raw mode was never successfully entered.

initEditor() (called once in main(), before enableRawMode()) sets every editorConfig field to its startup value — cx=cy=rowoff=coloff=0, numrows=0, row=NULL, dirty=0, filename=NULL, syntax=NULL — then calls updateWindowSize() (which fills screenrows/screencols via an ioctl-based query and immediately does `E.screenrows -= 2;` to reserve two lines for the status bar) and registers `signal(SIGWINCH, handleSigWinCh)` so a live terminal resize re-runs updateWindowSize() and re-clamps the cursor. Notably initEditor() does NOT set E.rawmode — enableRawMode() is what later sets that field, once it actually succeeds.

E.dirty starts at 0 and is incremented by every mutating operation (editorInsertChar, editorDelChar, editorInsertNewline, editorInsertRow, editorDelRow, etc., each call `E.dirty++`), and is only reset to 0 inside editorSave() after a successful write. editorProcessKeypress()'s CTRL_Q handler reads `E.dirty` (as a boolean-ish truthiness check) together with a function-local `quit_times` countdown to decide whether Ctrl-Q needs to be pressed multiple times before exit(0).

E.statusmsg / E.statusmsg_time are written together, only by editorSetStatusMessage(const char *fmt, ...) — a printf-style variadic function that formats into the fixed 80-byte buffer with vsnprintf() and stamps `E.statusmsg_time = time(NULL)`. The renderer (editorRefreshScreen(), covered in Chapter 06) reads statusmsg_time to decide whether the message is still fresh enough to show, so the timestamp is state in its own right, not just metadata.

E.syntax is a pointer into a static, compile-time table (HLDB, an array of `struct editorSyntax`) rather than owned/allocated memory — editorSelectSyntaxHighlight() sets it by address-of into that table (or leaves it NULL for an unrecognized file type), so editorConfig never has to free it.

pitfalls: It's tempting to assume E must be heap-allocated or passed around like a typical 'context' object in larger programs; it isn't. It's a single plain file-scope struct, zero-initialized by the C runtime itself before main(), then explicitly re-zeroed field-by-field in initEditor() — there is no malloc() and no free() for E itself (only for E.row and the strings it points to).

E.rawmode looks like it belongs with document-state fields like dirty or filename, but it tracks terminal state, not file state — conflating the two is easy to do when skimming the struct top-to-bottom, but they have different lifetimes and different owners (one call site each: enableRawMode/disableRawMode vs. the many editor* mutators).

erow and struct editorSyntax are declared right above editorConfig and are easy to mentally fold into 'the' editor state struct — they aren't part of it. E.row is a pointer to an array of erow, and E.syntax is a pointer into a separate static table; editorConfig itself owns none of their memory outright except the pointers.

Because `static struct editorConfig E;` is a bare global with internal linkage, nothing stops any function in the file from mutating any field directly — there's no getter/setter discipline. That's a deliberate simplicity trade-off for a ~1000-line single-file program, not a pattern to imitate unmodified in a larger codebase (Chapter 19, Refactoring Kilo, returns to this).

claims.text: There is no `KILO_TAB_STOP` (or any similarly named) constant anywhere in antirez's shipped kilo.c — a full-file search finds zero matches. The tab width of 8 columns is a bare numeric literal, repeated at three separate call sites (twice in editorUpdateRow, once more in the cursor-column math inside editorRefreshScreen). This differs from the popular snaptoken 'Build Your Own Text Editor' derivative tutorial, which does define a named KILO_TAB_STOP constant — a reader who goes looking for it in the real repository won't find it.

editorUpdateRow(erow *row) is the single function that rebuilds row->render from row->chars, and it is called after every mutation to a row's characters (from editorInsertRow, editorRowInsertChar, editorRowAppendString, editorRowDelChar). It frees the old render buffer, counts how many TAB bytes are in chars to size a fresh allocation (worst case 8 bytes per tab, `row->size + tabs*8 + nonprint*9 + 1`), then walks chars a second time: on hitting a non-TAB byte it copies it straight into render, and on hitting a TAB it writes at least one space and then pads with more spaces (`while((idx+1) % 8 != 0)`) until render's write cursor lands on a column that is a multiple of 8. It ends by calling editorUpdateSyntax(row), so every content edit also refreshes that row's highlight bytes in the same pass.

editorUpdateRow's allocation-size formula includes a `nonprint` variable and a `nonprint*9` term, but nonprint is declared, initialized to 0, and never incremented anywhere in the function or the rest of the file — it is dead code that always contributes zero. (Non-printable characters are instead given special visual treatment later, at draw time in editorRefreshScreen, via the HL_NONPRINT highlight class — not by reserving extra bytes in render up front.) Before the allocation, kilo.c also guards against absurdly long lines: if the computed size as an unsigned long long would exceed UINT32_MAX, it prints "Some line of the edited file is too long for kilo" and calls exit(1) rather than risk an integer-overflow-driven under-allocation.

editorInsertRow(int at, char *s, size_t len) grows E.row by exactly one erow slot per call via realloc(E.row, sizeof(erow)*(E.numrows+1)) — an O(n) reallocation on every single line inserted, meaning loading an n-line file costs O(n^2) row-array work in the worst case, a deliberate simplicity-over-scalability trade consistent with Kilo's small-file, small-program design goal. When inserting anywhere but the very end (at != E.numrows), it opens a gap by memmove(E.row+at+1, E.row+at, sizeof(E.row[0])*(E.numrows-at)) — shifting every row at or after `at` one slot toward higher memory — and then manually renumbers the .idx field of every shifted row: `for (int j = at+1; j <= E.numrows; j++) E.row[j].idx++;`. It then deep-copies the caller's bytes with `E.row[at].chars = malloc(len+1); memcpy(E.row[at].chars, s, len+1);` (copying len+1 bytes, i.e. including one byte past `len` that the caller must guarantee is a valid null terminator), zeroes render/hl/rsize/hl_oc so editorUpdateRow will build them fresh, sets `E.row[at].idx = at`, calls editorUpdateRow(E.row+at), and only then increments E.numrows and E.dirty.

editorFreeRow(erow *row) releases exactly the three heap buffers one erow owns — render, chars, hl — via three free() calls, but does not free the erow struct itself, because erows live inline inside the single E.row array rather than being individually heap-allocated. editorDelRow(int at) calls editorFreeRow on the target row, closes the gap with memmove(E.row+at, E.row+at+1, sizeof(E.row[0])*(E.numrows-at-1)) (the mirror-image direction of editorInsertRow's memmove), decrements E.numrows and increments E.dirty — but the array's allocated capacity is never realloc'd smaller; only the logical numrows count shrinks.

editorDelRow's own idx-renumbering loop reads `for (int j = at; j < E.numrows-1; j++) E.row[j].idx++;` — incrementing the .idx field of every row that just shifted down into a lower array position, the same direction editorInsertRow uses to shift rows *up* out of the way of a new insertion. Since .idx is read elsewhere (editorUpdateSyntax uses row->idx to look up a row's immediate predecessor/successor for multi-line-comment-state propagation, at row->idx-1 and row->idx+1), incrementing rather than decrementing after a deletion looks like a genuine indexing bug in the shipped source, not a stylistic choice — worth verifying against a fresh clone rather than taking on faith, and a good source of a 'even small, celebrated codebases ship real bugs' teaching moment.

editorRowInsertChar(erow *row, int at, int c) has two branches. If `at > row->size` (the caller wants to place a character past the current end of the line — this is how Kilo lets the cursor sit and type past the last existing column), it pads the gap with literal space characters: reallocates for `size+padlen+2` bytes (kilo.c's own comment: "+2 means: new char and null term"), memset()s `padlen` spaces starting at the old end, places a null terminator, and grows `size` by `padlen+1`. Otherwise (inserting inside or at the exact end of existing content) it reallocates for `size+2` bytes and opens a one-byte gap with `memmove(chars+at+1, chars+at, size-at+1)` (the `+1` deliberately carries the existing null terminator forward too), then grows `size` by one. Either branch finishes by writing `chars[at] = c`, calling editorUpdateRow(row), and incrementing E.dirty.

editorRowAppendString(erow *row, char *s, size_t len) reallocates chars to `size+len+1`, memcpy()s the new bytes onto the existing end, grows size by len, writes a fresh null terminator, calls editorUpdateRow, and increments E.dirty — this is the exact primitive later chapters use to implement 'Backspace at column zero joins the current line onto the end of the previous line'.

editorRowDelChar(erow *row, int at) closes a one-byte gap with `memmove(chars+at, chars+at+1, size-at)` (the length `size-at` includes the trailing null terminator, so it gets shifted down too), then calls editorUpdateRow(row) and only afterward decrements row->size — the reverse order from editorRowInsertChar/editorRowAppendString, which both update size *before* calling editorUpdateRow. Because editorUpdateRow reads row->size to know how far to walk row->chars, this ordering means the row is re-rendered once using the pre-decrement size value against already-shifted bytes; the stale duplicate byte at the old last position is simply harmless leftover that the next real read/write ignores, but it is a visible inconsistency in an otherwise-consistent pattern across the three row-mutation functions.

editorRowsToString(int *buflen) flattens the entire E.row array back into one contiguous heap buffer for saving, using row->chars only — never row->render. It makes two passes: the first sums `E.row[j].size + 1` across every row (the +1 reserving one byte for a '\n' appended after every row, including the last) into totlen, allocates totlen+1 bytes (the extra byte for a final null terminator) and reports totlen (excluding that null terminator) back through *buflen; the second pass memcpy()s each row's raw chars bytes, then manually appends a literal '\n' after each one. Because it copies chars (not render), tab characters are written back to disk exactly as the single raw bytes they are — the space-expanded render buffer never touches the saved file.

kilo.c has no editorRowCxToRx/editorRowRxToCx function pair (confirmed absent by full-file search) — the conversion from a raw character index (E.cx, counted in bytes/characters of row->chars) to an on-screen rendered column exists in exactly one place, inlined directly inside editorRefreshScreen, right before it writes the final cursor-positioning escape sequence: a loop walks row->chars from the left edge of the viewport (E.coloff) up to the cursor's logical column (E.cx+E.coloff), and for every TAB byte it encounters, fast-forwards the running screen-column counter cx by `7-((cx)%8)` extra positions before the unconditional `cx++`. The comment directly above this loop states the reason explicitly: "the horizontal position at which the cursor is displayed may be different compared to 'E.cx' because of TABs." This is a second, independent implementation of the same 8-column tab math already done once inside editorUpdateRow — two places that would both need updating if the tab width ever changed, since neither refers to a shared constant.

The erow struct's hl field (one syntax-highlight byte per character) is deliberately sized and indexed against row->rsize / row->render, not row->size / row->chars — editorUpdateSyntax allocates it with `row->hl = realloc(row->hl, row->rsize)`. Because editorUpdateRow always calls editorUpdateSyntax(row) as its very last step, there is no code path that updates a row's raw text without also recomputing both its rendered form and its highlight bytes in the same call — text storage, tab rendering, and syntax highlighting are one coupled pipeline in Kilo, not three independent systems a caller has to remember to keep in sync by hand.

Across text-editor implementations generally, an array of per-line buffers like Kilo's is the simplest point on a well-known spectrum of buffer designs (array-of-lines, gap buffer, rope, piece table); it trades O(n) line insert/delete (an array memmove) and O(line length) character insert/delete (another memmove) for maximum implementation simplicity, which is a deliberate, reasonable choice at the file sizes and 'under ~1000 lines of C' scope Kilo targets — the same trade-off named in Chapter 02's research entry.

Verbatim cx-to-screen-column loop from inside editorRefreshScreen, kilo.c ~lines 980-992, fetched 2026-07-20. This is the only cx/render-column conversion in the file; there is no separate editorRowCxToRx function.

pitfalls: There is no KILO_TAB_STOP constant in the real kilo.c, unlike the popular snaptoken tutorial derivative — the tab width (8) is a bare literal repeated in three places (twice in editorUpdateRow, once in editorRefreshScreen's cursor-column math). Presenting a named constant as if it exists in antirez's source would be inventing an API the file doesn't have.

editorUpdateRow's `nonprint` variable and the `nonprint*9` term in its two allocation-size formulas are dead code — nonprint is never incremented anywhere in the file, so that term is always zero. Don't present it as functioning non-printable-character budgeting; that job is actually done later, at draw time, via the HL_NONPRINT highlight class.

editorDelRow's idx-renumbering loop (`for (int j = at; j < E.numrows-1; j++) E.row[j].idx++;`) increments the .idx of every row shifted down by a deletion, the same direction editorInsertRow uses to shift rows out of the way of an insertion. Since row->idx is read elsewhere for multi-line-comment-state lookups, this reads as a genuine bug in the shipped source rather than a deliberate design choice — worth flagging to readers as a real 'even celebrated small codebases ship bugs' example, not smoothing it over as intentional.

editorRowInsertChar's `at > row->size` branch (pad with spaces) is the actual location of 'what happens if you try to insert past the end of a line' — it is not handled in a cursor-movement function, and a reader looking for it in editorMoveCursor or similar won't find it there.

chars/size describe the raw, on-disk row; render/rsize describe a second, independently-allocated display buffer with tabs expanded to spaces (and, via hl, one highlight byte per rendered character). editorRowsToString (used for saving) reads only chars — never render — so tabs are always saved back as single raw tab bytes, never as the expanded spaces the screen shows.

The tab-to-screen-column math is implemented twice, independently: once inside editorUpdateRow (building render) and again inside editorRefreshScreen (positioning the terminal cursor via cx). Neither refers to a shared constant or shared helper function — a reader assuming there's one canonical 'tab width' code path to change would miss the second site.

editorRowDelChar decrements row->size *after* calling editorUpdateRow, the opposite order from editorRowInsertChar and editorRowAppendString (which update size first). The window this opens is harmless in practice, but it's a real inconsistency worth noting rather than glossing over as uniform across the three functions.

claims.text: E.cx/E.cy are not the cursor's position in the file — they are screen-relative coordinates, always kept inside [0, E.screencols) and [0, E.screenrows). The true file position is computed on demand wherever it's needed as filerow = E.rowoff+E.cy, filecol = E.coloff+E.cx; E.rowoff/E.coloff are the file row/column of the screen's top-left cell. Nearly every cursor-affecting function (editorMoveCursor, editorInsertChar, editorInsertNewline, editorDelChar) opens by recomputing filerow/filecol from cy/cx/rowoff/coloff before doing anything else.

kilo.c has no dedicated 'scroll' function — there is no editorScroll() anywhere in the file (confirmed by full-file search). Scrolling is folded directly into editorMoveCursor(int key): each of the four arrow-key branches decides for itself whether to move cy/cx (staying on the current screen) or to move rowoff/coloff instead (sliding the viewport), based on whether the cursor is already sitting at the edge of the visible screen.

editorMoveCursor's ARROW_LEFT case: if E.cx is already 0, first try to consume horizontal scroll (E.coloff--) before touching the row; only once coloff is also 0 does it move up a logical line, setting E.cy-- and E.cx = E.row[filerow-1].size (jumping to the end of the previous line — Kilo treats Left-at-column-0 as 'merge into the previous line's end', not a no-op), with a clamp (if E.cx > E.screencols-1) that shifts coloff to keep that new cx on screen if the previous line is wider than the viewport. If E.cx isn't 0, it's a plain E.cx -= 1 with no offset change.

editorMoveCursor's ARROW_RIGHT case only moves at all if a row exists at filerow. If filecol < row->size (still inside the line), it either bumps E.cx or, if E.cx is already at the last visible column (E.screencols-1), bumps E.coloff instead. If filecol == row->size exactly (the cursor is one position past the last character — the valid 'end of line' position, not an error), it wraps to the start of the next line: E.cx = 0, E.coloff = 0, and either E.cy++ or E.rowoff++ depending on whether the current screen row is the last visible one.

editorMoveCursor's ARROW_UP and ARROW_DOWN cases are simpler and do not wrap horizontally at all: ARROW_UP does E.rowoff-- when already at screen row 0 (E.cy==0), else E.cy--; ARROW_DOWN checks filerow < E.numrows (refusing to scroll past the last line of the file) and then does E.rowoff++ when at the last visible screen row (E.cy == E.screenrows-1), else E.cy++. Neither branch touches E.coloff or E.cx directly — any horizontal correction needed after landing on a shorter line happens in the shared clamp step that runs after the switch, not inside these cases.

After the key-specific switch, editorMoveCursor recomputes filerow/filecol/row/rowlen one more time and applies a single shared clamp: if filecol > rowlen (the cursor's file column is now past the end of whatever line it landed on), it pulls E.cx back by the overshoot, and if that drives E.cx negative, folds the remainder into E.coloff instead (E.coloff += E.cx; E.cx = 0). This is the mechanism that snaps the cursor to end-of-line when arrowing (up or down) from a long line onto a shorter one — kilo.c has no 'remembered' preferred column; the horizontal position is recomputed fresh, and clamped fresh, on every single move.

Page Up / Page Down are implemented in editorProcessKeypress, not editorMoveCursor, and not by directly reassigning E.rowoff to jump a screenful at once. The handler first snaps E.cy to the top (0) on PAGE_UP or the bottom (E.screenrows-1) on PAGE_DOWN if it isn't already there, then runs a loop `int times = E.screenrows; while(times--) editorMoveCursor(...)` calling the ordinary ARROW_UP or ARROW_DOWN case E.screenrows times in a row — a full page motion is literally E.screenrows individual single-line cursor moves executed back to back, reusing all of editorMoveCursor's existing offset/clamp logic rather than duplicating it.

editorReadKey() does parse Home and End as their own soft key codes: HOME_KEY and END_KEY (values 1000+, defined in the same enum as ARROW_LEFT/RIGHT/UP/DOWN/DEL_KEY/PAGE_UP/PAGE_DOWN), recognized from the escape sequences ESC [ H, ESC [ F, ESC O H, and ESC O F. But editorProcessKeypress's switch statement — confirmed by reading the full function — has no case for HOME_KEY or END_KEY. Both fall through to the switch's default branch, `editorInsertChar(c)`, which passes the raw integer key code (1005 or 1006) down to editorRowInsertChar, where it's assigned into a `char` array slot (`row->chars[at] = c;`) and silently truncated to one byte. In the shipped source, pressing Home or End does not move the cursor to the start or end of the line — it inserts one stray byte into the document instead.

initEditor() sets E.rowoff = E.coloff = 0 at startup (alongside E.cx = E.cy = 0, covered in Chapter 07), so the viewport always begins pinned to the very top-left of the file; updateWindowSize()/handleSigWinCh() (also Chapter 07) independently guard against a live terminal resize leaving E.cy or E.cx pointing past the newly-shrunk E.screenrows/E.screencols by clamping them back in bounds, but that resize-time clamp does not touch E.rowoff/E.coloff — only editorMoveCursor's per-key logic and the post-switch clamp adjust those.

snippets.note: Verbatim editorMoveCursor, kilo.c lines 1111-1178, fetched from raw.githubusercontent.com/antirez/kilo/master/kilo.c on 2026-07-20.
Verbatim PAGE_UP/PAGE_DOWN case from editorProcessKeypress, kilo.c ~lines 1223-1234, fetched 2026-07-20.

Verbatim (lightly excerpted) escape-sequence parsing from editorReadKey, kilo.c lines ~278-296, fetched 2026-07-20 -- shows HOME_KEY/END_KEY are genuinely parsed, which is what makes their absence from editorProcessKeypress's switch notable rather than expected.

pitfalls: Home and End are parsed by editorReadKey() into real HOME_KEY/END_KEY codes but have no case in editorProcessKeypress's switch in the shipped source -- they silently fall to the default branch and get inserted into the document as a truncated stray byte instead of moving the cursor. Don't present Home/End as working line-start/line-end shortcuts in Kilo; they're recognized but not wired up.

There is no editorScroll() function and no 'preferred column' memory in kilo.c. Scrolling is inline inside editorMoveCursor's four key-specific branches (offset changes only at the screen edge), and horizontal position after an up/down move is recomputed and clamped fresh every time via the shared post-switch clamp -- it does not remember where the cursor 'wanted' to be on a longer line you previously visited.

PAGE_UP/PAGE_DOWN do not jump E.rowoff by a screenful directly; they snap E.cy to an edge and then call editorMoveCursor() E.screenrows times in a loop, reusing the ordinary single-step arrow logic. A reader expecting a dedicated fast-path scroll implementation will not find one.
E.cx/E.cy are screen coordinates, not file coordinates -- always read filerow=E.rowoff+E.cy / filecol=E.coloff+E.cx when you need 'where in the document is the cursor', never E.cx/E.cy alone.

volatile: the exact line count / version tag of kilo.c on GitHub (currently ~1308 lines including license header, per cloc's stricter count antirez advertises 'less than 1K')
terminal-emulator-specific escape sequence quirks (xterm vs. other VT100-compatible emulators)
